metadata.course_name: Stanford CS336 Language Modeling from Scratch

discussions.title: Introduction to Model Building and Resource Efficiency
discussions.summary:
This section introduces the goals for building language models from scratch using PyTorch. It highlights the importance of understanding PyTorch basics like tensors, models, optimizers, and training loops, with a strong focus on resource efficiency, including memory and computation. The instructor motivates with example calculations estimating training time and resources needed for large models, teaching learners to do rough math quickly (called napkin math) to help plan training experiments.

Definitions:
- Napkin math: Simple, quick estimations for complex numbers or calculations to get a rough idea.
- Efficiency: Using less memory and computation to save time and money during model training.

Key Concepts:
- Training large models requires careful accounting of compute power (flops) and memory.
- The number six times parameters times tokens is a key formula estimating total floating point operations (flops) in training.
- It is important to do rough resource calculations before model training rather than just trial and error.

Important Notes:
- Efficiency can directly translate into reduced costs.
- Large-scale models involve careful planning of resources.
- Transformers will be covered separately later; this lecture focuses on simpler models and mechanics.

discussions.title: Understanding Tensors and Memory Usage in PyTorch
discussions.summary:
This part explains tensors, which are the main data structure in PyTorch to store data, parameters, gradients, and more. It details how tensors use floating-point numbers stored in memory and how the choice of floating-point format affects memory and speed.

Definitions:
- Tensor: A multi-dimensional array used to store data like parameters and inputs in deep learning.
- Float32 (FP32): Standard 32-bit floating point format with 1 sign bit, 8 bits exponent, and 23 bits fraction. Used for full precision in deep learning.
- Float16 (FP16): Half-precision 16-bit floating point format with less exponent and fraction bits, uses less memory but has smaller dynamic range.
- BFloat16 (BF16): 16-bit format designed for deep learning with same dynamic range as float32 but smaller precision, good for training stable models.
- FP8 (Float 8): Very low precision 8-bit floating point, newer and supported on advanced GPUs for faster computation but less stable.

Key Concepts:
- Float32 uses 4 bytes per number; tensor memory is number_of_elements × bytes_per_element.
- Lower precision formats save memory and increase speed but can cause problems like underflow or instability if too small.
- BF16 is preferred over FP16 for training because it has better dynamic range.
- Mixed precision training uses different precisions at different steps to balance speed and stability.

Important Notes:
- Float16 can cause underflow for very small numbers and be unstable for large models.
- Using mixed precision is a balance; parameters and optimizer states often stay in float32.
- New formats like FP8 offer exciting speed but require caution.
- Always be aware of tensor memory format to manage resources properly.

discussions.title: Tensors on CPU vs GPU and Memory Contiguity
discussions.summary:
This section covers where tensors are stored in PyTorch (CPU or GPU) and why it matters for speed and data movement. It also explains that tensors are pointers to memory with metadata like shape and strides. Showing how slicing or viewing tensors may share memory without copying, but some operations create copies. The idea of contiguous vs non-contiguous memory layout is introduced.

Definitions:
- CPU: Central Processing Unit, the main computer processor storing tensors by default.
- GPU: Graphics Processing Unit, specialized hardware for fast tensor computation requiring tensors to be moved explicitly.
- Contiguous tensor: A tensor with memory laid out so elements are stored consecutively, allowing easier reshaping and viewing.
- Non-contiguous tensor: A tensor with memory layout where elements are not stored consecutively, often after operations like transpose.
- Stride: Metadata telling how many elements to skip to move to the next element in each tensor dimension.

Key Concepts:
- Tensor variables point to memory; slicing often creates views sharing the same data without copying.
- Moving data from CPU to GPU is necessary for faster computation.
- Some tensor operations like transpose create non-contiguous tensors.
- Non-contiguous tensors cannot always be reshaped without copying, but contiguous tensors can.

Important Notes:
- Be careful when modifying a tensor view, as it can affect the original tensor.
- Using views saves memory and is normally free, but operations making data contiguous create copies.
- Always keep track of where your tensor is (CPU or GPU) to avoid slowdowns.
- Use assertions or commands in code to check tensor device for debugging.

discussions.title: Matrix Multiplication, Batching, and Einstein Summation (Einsum) Notation
discussions.summary:
This section discusses matrix multiplication, which is the core operation in deep learning models, especially for language models. It explains how operations are done on batches of data and sequences at once. It introduces the Einstein summation (einsum) notation, a clearer way of specifying tensor operations by naming dimensions instead of using confusing negative indices.

Definitions:
- Matrix Multiplication: Multiplying two matrices to produce a third, combining rows of one with columns of the other.
- Batch: Group of examples processed together to speed up training.
- Sequence: Ordered data points in language models, like words in a sentence.
- Einsum notation: A way to write tensor contractions by naming dimensions explicitly, making complex operations easier to read and less error-prone.

Key Concepts:
- Deep learning computations often happen over batch and sequence dimensions.
- E.g., multiplying a batch of sequences against a weight matrix.
- Einsum replaces indexing with readable dimension names, improves clarity and reduces bugs.
- Einsum can broadcast over multiple dimensions using '...'.

Important Notes:
- Using negative indices (like -1, -2) in PyTorch is common but confusing.
- Einsum notation helps document tensor shapes and operations clearly.
- Einsum compiles into efficient code and should be used once at compile time.
- Learners are encouraged to explore tutorials on einsum for better understanding.

discussions.title: Changing Tensor Shapes with Einsum and Rearrange
discussions.summary:
This part shows how sometimes tensor dimensions represent multiple concepts combined (like heads and hidden sizes in transformers). It demonstrates how to split (unpack) these combined dimensions and then operate on them, finally flattening (packing) them back. The rearrange operation is introduced as a flexible way to change tensor shapes without copying data.

Definitions:
- Rearrange: A function to change the shape of a tensor by splitting or combining dimensions without copying data.
- Flattening: Combining multiple dimensions into a single dimension.

Key Concepts:
- Tensors can encode multiple aspects in a single dimension.
- Changing shapes is key to performing correct operations on complex models like transformers.
- Rearrange is more general and readable than basic view functions.

Important Notes:
- Rearrange helps when dealing with model components like attention heads.
- While not required, understanding these shape manipulations helps with assignment and model building.

discussions.title: Floating Point Operations (Flops) and Hardware Performance
discussions.summary:
Here, the lecture explains floating point operations (flops), which count basic math operations like additions and multiplications. It clarifies the difference between lowercase flop (number of operations) and uppercase FLOPS (operations per second), which measures hardware speed. It gives examples of flop counts for big models and discusses how hardware specs like A100 or H100 GPUs translate to computational power.

Definitions:
- Flop (floating point operation): One basic math operation (addition or multiplication) with floating point numbers.
- FLOPS (floating point operations per second): How many floating point operations a computer can do in one second.
- Sparsity: Having many zero values in matrices, which can speed up computation if supported.

Key Concepts:
- Counting flops helps estimate model training computation.
- Modern GPUs have peak FLOPS based on precision and support for sparsity.
- Real achievable FLOPS depend on how dense the matrices are and actual code efficiency.

Important Notes:
- Sparsity speedups only apply under specific structured zero patterns.
- Marketing numbers often show peak FLOPS with sparsity; real use may be half or less.
- Always benchmark real performance rather than rely only on spec sheets.

discussions.title: Simple Linear Models and Flop Counting for Matrix Multiplication
discussions.summary:
This section presents a basic linear model example and explains how to count the floating point operations (flops) involved. It shows the formula for flops in matrix multiplication as two times the product of the matrix dimensions. This applies well to deep learning where matrix multiplication dominates the compute cost.

Definitions:
- Linear Model: A function mapping input vectors to output vectors by multiplying with a weight matrix.
- Data point: One sample/input in the dataset.

Key Concepts:
- Matrix multiplication flop count = 2 × (rows) × (shared_dim) × (columns).
- In deep learning, the total flops roughly equal two times the number of tokens times the number of parameters.
- Other operations cost less compared to large matrix multiplications.

Important Notes:
- The formula is approximate and ignores optimizations and hardware details.
- For larger models, matrix multiplication is the main compute bottleneck.
- Understanding flop counts helps estimate model training time and cost.

discussions.title: Practical Timing and Model Flop Utilization (MFU)
discussions.summary:
This topic covers how to measure actual time for matrix multiplication on GPUs and how to compare it to hardware peak speeds using the concept of Model Flop Utilization (MFU). MFU measures how efficiently your code uses the GPU compared to theoretical maximums.

Definitions:
- Model Flop Utilization (MFU): Ratio of actual useful floating point operations performed per second to the peak theoretical FLOPS of the hardware.

Key Concepts:
- Real GPU speeds are usually lower than peak specs due to overhead and other factors.
- Good MFU is above 0.5; very low MFU indicates inefficient hardware usage.
- Using mixed precision (like BF16 instead of FP32) can improve speed but sometimes lowers MFU.

Important Notes:
- MFU measures model compute efficiency, not just hardware raw speed.
- Don't trust peak FLOPS alone; always benchmark your model code.
- Tensor cores on GPUs provide faster matrix multiplication and are used by default in PyTorch with compilation.

discussions.title: Gradients and Backpropagation Flops in Linear Models
discussions.summary:
This section explains the additional computation required for the backward pass in training, where gradients of loss with respect to weights and activations are computed by applying the chain rule. For a simple linear model, the backward pass requires about twice the flops of the forward pass, making total training cost about six times the product of parameters and data points.

Definitions:
- Gradient: The derivative of the loss function with respect to model parameters, used to update parameters.
- Backward pass: The phase in training computing gradients using chain rule.

Key Concepts:
- Forward pass flops = 2 × parameters × data points.
- Backward pass flops = 4 × parameters × data points.
- Total training flops roughly = 6 × parameters × data points.
- Computing gradients involves matrix multiplications similar to forward pass.

Important Notes:
- This analysis uses simple models but generalizes to many deep learning models.
- In homework, you use PyTorch's automatic differentiation, not manual gradient calculations.
- Understanding flop counts helps in resource estimation and efficiency.

discussions.title: Memory Requirements: Parameters, Activations, Gradients, and Optimizer States
discussions.summary:
This part details memory usage in training deep learning models. It covers how much memory is needed to store model parameters, activations (intermediate outputs), gradients, and optimizer states. It explains that optimizer states store extra info like running averages in adaptive optimizers and that activations must be saved for backward pass unless special tricks are used.

Definitions:
- Parameters: Weights of the model.
- Activations: Outputs of each layer during the forward pass.
- Optimizer States: Extra information stored by optimizers (e.g., running averages of gradients).
- Activation Checkpointing: Technique to save memory by recomputing activations instead of storing all.

Key Concepts:
- Memory for training = memory for parameters + activations + gradients + optimizer states.
- Activations depend on batch size, number of layers, and dimensions.
- Optimizer states generally require as much memory as parameters.

Important Notes:
- Storing activations is necessary for computing gradients, but can be optimized.
- Proper memory accounting is important for working with large models.
- Deep learning literature provides standard formulas (like Xavier initialization) for stable parameter initialization.

discussions.title: Model Building and Parameter Initialization in PyTorch
discussions.summary:
This section discusses how to create models and parameters in PyTorch, focusing on the importance of good parameter initialization. Poor initialization can cause output values to explode, making training unstable. Xavier initialization rescales parameters to keep values stable, commonly used in deep learning.

Definitions:
- Xavier Initialization: A way to initialize weights by scaling them so the variance of outputs is stable, usually dividing by square root of input size.

Key Concepts:
- Initializing parameters with too large values causes unstable outputs.
- Scaling parameters by 1/sqrt(input dimension) keeps outputs stable.
- Truncation can be used to avoid extreme values.

Important Notes:
- Stable initialization avoids training problems.
- Good parameter startup is critical for large models.
- Initialization methods are well-studied and widely used.

discussions.title: Randomness and Reproducibility in Model Training
discussions.summary:
This short section highlights the challenges of randomness in model training, such as different initializations, data shuffling, and dropout. Setting random seeds for each source of randomness helps make experiments reproducible and debugging easier.

Definitions:
- Random Seed: A fixed starting point for random number generation to ensure reproducible results.

Key Concepts:
- Multiple sources of randomness affect model behavior.
- Fixing seeds helps produce the same outputs in repeated experiments.

Important Notes:
- Use different seeds for each randomness source to test effects independently.
- Determinism is useful when debugging model issues.

discussions.title: Data Loading Techniques for Large Datasets
discussions.summary:
The lecture briefly covers how language model data is stored as long sequences of token IDs, which are serialized using memory-mapped (mmap) files. This technique allows handling huge datasets (terabytes) without loading all data into memory, by loading parts only when needed.

Definitions:
- Memmap (Memory Map): A way to map data files to memory so parts can be loaded on demand without reading the entire file.

Key Concepts:
- Data loading needs to be efficient for very large datasets.
- Using memmaps allows scalable batch sampling without huge RAM usage.

Important Notes:
- Avoid loading entire datasets into RAM to prevent crashes.
- Memmaps help build efficient data loaders for training.

discussions.title: Optimizers: Overview and Implementing Adagrad in PyTorch
discussions.summary:
This section reviews different optimizers: SGD (Stochastic Gradient Descent), Momentum, Adagrad, RMSProp, and Adam. It introduces the idea of maintaining running averages of gradients or squared gradients to improve training. The implementation of Adagrad optimizer is shown as an example, focusing on how optimizer states are stored and updated.

Definitions:
- Optimizer: Algorithm that updates model parameters to reduce loss based on gradients.
- Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD): Basic optimizer moving parameters in direction of gradient.
- Momentum: Optimizer that smooths updates using a running average of past gradients.
- Adagrad: Optimizer scaling learning rates based on past squared gradients.
- RMSProp: Improvement on Adagrad using exponential moving average of squared gradients.
- Adam: Combines momentum and RMSProp for adaptive updates.

Key Concepts:
- Optimizers maintain state like running averages to adjust learning steps.
- In PyTorch, optimizer state is a dictionary mapping parameters to stored values.
- Optimizer step applies update rules using gradients and state.

Important Notes:
- Homework requires implementing Adam, but Adagrad is shown here.
- Clearing optimizer state memory can be important for large models and parallelism.
- Understanding optimizer workings helps debugging and customization.

discussions.title: Training Loop Basics and Checkpointing
discussions.summary:
This part presents the basic training loop: forward pass, backward pass for gradients, and optimizer step to update parameters. It emphasizes the importance of saving model and optimizer states periodically (checkpointing) during long training to avoid losing progress in case of crashes.

Definitions:
- Training Loop: The process of repeatedly running forward and backward passes, then updating parameters.
- Checkpointing: Saving the current state of training including model parameters and optimizer to disk.

Key Concepts:
- Training can take a long time and be interrupted unexpectedly.
- Saving checkpoints allows resuming training without starting over.

Important Notes:
- Save model, optimizer, and iteration count together.
- Checkpoint frequency balances safety and overhead.

discussions.title: Mixed Precision Training and Numerical Stability
discussions.summary:
The lecture discusses trade-offs in choosing numerical precision for training. Higher precision (float32) is more stable but slower and uses more memory, while lower precision (BF16 or FP8) is faster and smaller but less stable. Mixed precision combines formats to gain speed without sacrificing stability. Also, training uses higher precision, while inference can use much lower precision (quantization).

Definitions:
- Mixed Precision Training: Using different data types at different steps for faster, efficient training without losing much accuracy.
- Quantization: Reducing precision of a trained model for faster and smaller inference.

Key Concepts:
- Recent hardware supports lower precision formats to speed up training.
- Designing models for hardware constraints helps efficiency.
- Training at low precision is challenging due to numerical instability.
- Post-training quantization is easier and widely used for inference.

Important Notes:
- PyTorch offers tools to simplify mixed precision training.
- Model and system design increasingly depend on hardware capabilities.
- Experimentation and tricks are often needed for stable low-precision training.

discussions.title: Summary and Next Steps
discussions.summary:
The class wraps up covering PyTorch basics from tensors to training loops, memory and flop accounting for simple models. The instructor hopes students will solidify these ideas by applying them to transformers in assignment one.

Key Concepts:
- Practical understanding of PyTorch primitives is key.
- Memory and compute tracking ensures efficient model training.
- Applying these concepts to transformers will reinforce learning.

Important Notes:
- Students are encouraged to review material thoroughly and practice.
- Next time, deeper exploration into model architectures will continue.
